name: Digital Markets Act
summary:
- The Digital Markets Act (Regulation (EU) 2022/1925) lays down harmonised rules ensuring contestable and fair markets in the digital sector across the Union where gatekeepers are present, for the benefit of business users and end users.
- It applies to core platform services provided by designated gatekeepers to business users or end users in the Union, irrespective of where the gatekeeper is established.
- Designated gatekeepers must comply with the obligations in Articles 5, 6 and 7, demonstrate compliance, report, appoint an independent compliance function and inform the Commission of concentrations.
- The Commission enforces the regime through market investigations, proceedings, inspections, and may impose fines up to 10% of total worldwide turnover for non-compliance.
scope: The Regulation applies to core platform services provided or offered by gatekeepers to business users established in the Union or end users established or located in the Union, irrespective of the place of establishment or residence of the gatekeepers (Article 1). 'Core platform services' include online intermediation services, online search engines, online social networking services, video-sharing platform services, number-independent interpersonal communications services, operating systems, web browsers, virtual assistants, cloud computing services and online advertising services (Article 2). An undertaking is designated as a gatekeeper where it has a significant impact on the internal market, provides a core platform service which is an important gateway for business users to reach end users, and enjoys an entrenched and durable position, with quantitative presumptions including an annual Union turnover of EUR 7.5 billion (Article 3). The General Court has confirmed the application of the designation requirements and presumptions, including in respect of online social networking services (Case T-1077/23).
process: The Commission designates gatekeepers under Article 3 and may reconsider, amend or repeal a designation, reviewing status at least every 3 years (Article 4). It may open market investigations to designate gatekeepers (Article 17), examine systematic non-compliance (Article 18), or examine new services and practices (Article 19), each opened by a decision under Article 16. The Commission may open proceedings (Article 20), issue requests for information (Article 21), conduct interviews (Article 22) and inspections (Article 23). It can adopt interim measures (Article 24), make commitments binding (Article 25) and monitor obligations (Article 26). Third parties may inform the Commission or national competent authorities of practices (Article 27). Enforcement is coordinated with national authorities (Article 37), national competition authorities through the European Competition Network (Article 38), and national courts (Article 39), supported by a high-level group (Article 40). Affected parties have the right to be heard and to access the file (Article 34).
consequence: Where the Commission finds non-compliance it adopts a non-compliance decision (Article 29). It may impose fines not exceeding 10% of total worldwide turnover in the preceding financial year for failure to comply with obligations under Articles 5, 6 and 7, measures under Article 8(2), remedies under Article 18(1), interim measures under Article 24, or binding commitments under Article 25 (Article 30). The Commission may also impose periodic penalty payments not exceeding 5% of average daily worldwide turnover per day to compel compliance (Article 31). The imposition of penalties is subject to a 5 year limitation period (Article 32), as is the enforcement of penalties (Article 33). In cases of systematic non-compliance identified through a market investigation, the Commission may impose behavioural or structural remedies (Article 18).

obligations.text: The gatekeeper shall comply with all obligations set out in Article 5 with respect to each core platform service, including not processing personal data of end users for online advertising services from third parties using its core platform services and not combining personal data across services.

The gatekeeper shall comply with the obligations in Article 6 (susceptible of further specification under Article 8), including not using non-publicly available data generated by business users in competition with them.
A gatekeeper providing number-independent interpersonal communications services shall make the basic functionalities interoperable with those of another provider, upon request and free of charge.

The gatekeeper shall ensure and demonstrate compliance with the obligations laid down in Articles 5, 6 and 7, ensuring measures are effective and comply with applicable law including Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

Within 6 months after designation, the gatekeeper shall provide the Commission with a detailed report on measures implemented to ensure compliance, publish a non-confidential summary, and update both at least annually.
An undertaking shall not segment, divide, subdivide, fragment or split its core platform services through any means in order to circumvent the quantitative thresholds in Article 3(2).
A gatekeeper shall inform the Commission of any intended concentration involving entities providing core platform services or other digital sector services, prior to its implementation.

Within 6 months after designation, a gatekeeper shall submit an independently audited description of any techniques for profiling of consumers applied across its core platform services and make a public overview available.

Gatekeepers shall introduce a compliance function independent from operational functions, composed of one or more compliance officers with sufficient authority, stature, resources and access to the management body.

guidance: Undertakings approaching the quantitative thresholds in Article 3(2) should assess in advance whether they may be designated as gatekeepers and avoid any segmentation that could be treated as circumvention under Article 13. Designated gatekeepers should establish an independent compliance function early (Article 28), prepare detailed compliance reports and the audited profiling description within the 6-month deadlines (Articles 11 and 15), and ensure measures are effective and consistent with data-protection and other applicable law (Article 8). Where compliance with a specific obligation threatens economic viability or raises public health or security concerns, gatekeepers may submit reasoned requests for suspension (Article 9) or exemption (Article 10). Gatekeepers facing proceedings should engage their right to be heard and access the file (Article 34) and may consider offering commitments during Article 18 proceedings (Article 25). Note that the General Court has upheld application of the designation requirements and presumptions, including their rebuttal and rights of the defence (Case T-1077/23), so robust evidence is needed to challenge a designation.
